An experimental study of ion diffusivity in graphene oxide membranes in single and binary salt solutions Melike B. Altay Geren , George Q. Chen , Donghyuck Park , Amanda V. Ellis , Sandra E. Kentish * Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria, 3010, Australia

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Graphene oxidePermeabilitySolubilityDiffusion coefficientIon transport

citation.title: An experimental study of ion diffusivity in graphene oxide membranes in single and binary salt solutions

content: A B S T R A C T Graphene oxide membranes (GOMs) are a promising material for next-generation membrane technologies. However, the mechanisms underlying their ion transport remain unclear, limiting the ability to optimise their design for specific applications. In this study, we systematically quantify ion transport by calculating the indi­vidual ion diffusion coefficients of GOMs equilibrated in LiCl, NaCl, KCl, and MgCl2 and binary mixtures of the monovalent salts across varied external salt concentrations. We demonstrate that counter-ion diffusion co­efficients generally remain unaffected by external salt concentration, while chloride co-ion diffusion coefficients increase with concentration up to ~0.3 M before plateauing, primarily influenced by electrostatic interactions between the membrane fixed charge groups and ions, rather than membrane water content. Interestingly, the diffusion coefficients of Na+ and Cl-ions in the GOMs are comparable to those in polymeric membranes, challenging previous reports of significantly enhanced ion transport in GOMs. Rather, our analysis reveals that ion permeability in GOMs is predominantly dictated by solubility effects rather than diffusion. In monovalent binary salt systems, counter-ion diffusion coefficients were generally lower than those of single-salt systems, while chloride diffusion coefficients remained comparable, reflecting the influence of ion interactions and membrane hydration on ion transport.

citation.title: An experimental study of ion diffusivity in graphene oxide membranes in single and binary salt solutions

content: 1. Introduction Membranes that combine high selectivity for separating ions and small molecules with exceptional flux have the potential to revolutionize the energy, water, and chemical industries, serving as a key driver for advancing sustainability and addressing critical global challenges [1,2]. Graphene oxide membranes (GOMs) are a promising candidate for next-generation membrane technologies due to their reportedly ultrafast water transport [3–6] and highly selective molecular and ionic sieving properties [7–13]. Despite their promising potential, GOMs face signif­icant challenges, including structural instability, swelling, and me­chanical deformation under aqueous and saline conditions [14–16]. Additionally, for water treatment applications such as desalination, practical benefits may remain limited since increasing permeability alone can provide only minimal energetic gains [17]. Nevertheless, understanding the intrinsic transport behaviour of GOMs remains crucial. Ion permeability through a membrane depends both upon the uptake of ions within the structure (referred to as the partition coefficient, the solubility or the activity) and their transport rate (diffusivity) [18,19]. Numerous studies have reported enhanced ion permeability in GOMs [8,20–22], often attributing it to structural characteristics such as inter­layer spacing [3] and channel alignment [23], or physicochemical properties such as surface charge [10].

citation.title: An experimental study of ion diffusivity in graphene oxide membranes in single and binary salt solutions

content: t to structural characteristics such as inter­layer spacing [3] and channel alignment [23], or physicochemical properties such as surface charge [10]. While these features likely play a role on fast ion permeation in GOMs, the independent parameters of solubility and diffusivity of ions within GOMs nanochannels remains poorly understood, leaving the mechanisms controlling ion transport unclear. While some studies have acknowledged these mechanisms, experimental validation is particularly lacking, with much of the exist­ing understanding based on molecular dynamic (MD) simulations [3,5,24–29]. For example, Joshi et al. first demonstrated ultrafast ion transport through GOMs, suggesting salt uptake as a possible contributor but no direct sorption measurements were provided [8]. Abraham et al. and Zhang et al. focused on tuning interlayer spacing and aligning channels to enhance ion permeability, relying on MD simulations without experimentally probing sorption [3,23]. Zhang et al. and Zhang et al. investigated ion selectivity in modified GOMs and evaluated transport through bulk salt permeability measurements [10,12].

content: However, neither study considered ion partitioning explicitly; both assumed equal ion concentrations inside and outside the membrane, without measuring or modelling ion sorption within the membrane phase. Notably, Zhang et al. highlighted the role of partition coefficients in facilitating K+ ion transport, yet provided no experimental data on ion sorption, focusing only on pure water sorption [12]. Similarly, Hong et al. studied ion selectivity in GOMs by evaluating ion permeability under the same assumption of uniform concentration, without ac­counting for partitioning effects [30]. Moreover, Cho et al. showed that ion permeability may depend more on sorption than on diffusivity, but their work was limited to salt solutions and did not consider the solu­bility of individual ions [31]. In our own recent work, we showed that ion solubility in GOMs is governed by both electrostatic attractions be­tween negatively charged functional groups and counter-ions, and non-electrostatic chemical interactions with neutral sites [32]. While these studies offer valuable insights into GOM performance, they do not provide direct experimental evidence linking sorption to the transport of individual ions. Given the remarkable sorption capabilities of GO for various ions [33–40], incorporating this parameter into ion transport studies is essential for a more comprehensive understanding of the transport mechanisms.

Various experimental techniques have been used to measure ion diffusion coefficients in membrane systems. For self-diffusion co­efficients, methods such as nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spec­troscopy [41–44], neutron scattering [43,45–48], and isotope labelling [18,49–51] are commonly employed. These techniques typically mea­sure diffusion under near-equilibrium conditions, yielding self-diffusion coefficients that reflect ion movement without the influence of external driving forces [18]. However, for practical applications and process modelling, it is essential to obtain ion diffusion coefficients under operational conditions where external forces are present, as these values more accurately represent ion transport in real-world membrane-based technologies [18,52]. For this purpose, diffusion cell experiments are frequently utilized. In these experiments however, the diffusion coeffi­cient is commonly calculated by assuming the same ion concentration within the membrane as within the external solution [8,12,53]. This approach overlooks the impact of solubility on permeability. This poses a significant limitation which may result in inaccurate evaluations of ion transport within GOMs nanochannels.

content: solubility on permeability. This poses a significant limitation which may result in inaccurate evaluations of ion transport within GOMs nanochannels. Conductivity measurements can also be used to offer insights into counter-ion diffusion under an applied electric field [15,52,54]. However, this method is only accurate when the membrane is equilibrated with pure water, ensuring that counter-ions are the sole mobile species [52] in order to exclude the co-ion term in the conductivity equation [54]. Indeed, both techniques often fail to differentiate between counter-ion and co-ion diffusivity, especially in membranes containing multiple ionic species. In this study, diffusion cell experiments and conductivity measure­ments are used in combination with the solubility data from our prior work [32] to estimate the individual diffusion coefficients of counterand co-ions in free-standing GOMs. In both the concentration-driven and electrically driven experiments, the primary transport mechanism is assumed to be diffusion, independent of the applied driving force [52]. The Einstein relation, which links ionic mobility to the diffusion coef­ficient, is regarded as valid. Although this relationship was originally derived for dilute aqueous solutions [55], it has also been applied to graphene-based materials in previous studies [27,56].

Our work isolates single-ion behaviour, quantifies their corresponding diffusion co­efficients and explicitly examines the contribution of sorption, addressing a key gap in the current understanding of ion transport in GOMs. To further explore ion transport behaviour, we also conduct com­plementary experiments using binary monovalent salt mixtures in a 1:1 ratio, allowing an assessment of how ion diffusion trends are influenced by the presence of competing ionic species. This combined approach allows for a more precise characterization of ion transport mechanisms, addressing a gap in the current literature and providing valuable data for the development of advanced GO-based membrane technologies. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Materials A 1 wt% graphene oxide dispersion (Standard Graphene, Korea) with a carbon-to-oxygen (C/O) ratio of 2.1 was used to fabricate GO mem­branes. Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.7 %), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.0 %), and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2⋅6H2O, 99.0 %), magnesium sulphate heptahydrate (MgSO4⋅7H2O, 99.0 %) were ob­tained from Chem-Supply, Australia. Lithium sulphate monohydrate (Li2SO4⋅H2O, 99 %) and sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, 99 %) were sourced from Sigma Aldrich, Australia. Potassium sulphate (K2SO4, 99 %) was purchased from Ajax Finechem.

content: 9 %) and sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, 99 %) were sourced from Sigma Aldrich, Australia. Potassium sulphate (K2SO4, 99 %) was purchased from Ajax Finechem. All solutions were prepared using deionized water (>18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore Synergy). Monovalent binary salt solutions were prepared by mixing two salts in equal equivalents amounts (1:1 ratio), so that their combined concentration matched the total equivalent concentration used in single salt solutions (e.g. 0.15 M LiCl +0.15 M NaCl results in the same total ion concentration as 0.3 M LiCl or 0.3 M NaCl). 2.2. GOMs preparation The GO dispersion was shaken for 3–4 min prior to use, with no additional purification. Several millilitres were applied onto poly­vinylidene difluoride (PVDF) substrates (220 * 370 * 3 mm, Profes­sional Plastics, Singapore) using a doctor blade, with the blade gap set to 2000 μm and a movement speed of 0.1 mm/s. After deposition, the films were dried at room temperature for two days, then heated at 60 ◦C for 1–2 h and carefully detached from the substrate. The freestanding membranes underwent further drying at 60 ◦C overnight and were stored in a controlled humidity environment (~50 %). The final dry membrane thickness was approximately 13 μm and the interlayer spacing was 0.89 nm. Further details of the GOMs preparation and characterization are provided in our earlier work [32]. 2.3. Ion solubility Binary salt solubilities were determined following the procedures established in our previous work [32].

content: in our earlier work [32]. 2.3. Ion solubility Binary salt solubilities were determined following the procedures established in our previous work [32]. In brief, cation concentrations were measured using an ashing method, where equilibrated membranes were dried, combusted at 500–550 ◦C and the resulting ash was dis­solved in 2 wt% nitric acid. The solution was then analysed by induc­tively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES -Agilent ICP 5100) to determine ion concentrations. Co-ion (chloride) concen­trations were determined via a desorption technique, in which salt-equilibrated membranes were soaked in deionized water and the released anions (chloride) were quantified using ion chromatography (IC - Dionex Aquion). 2.4. Ion permeation in GOMs Concentration driven ion permeation tests were carried out using a typical diffusion cell set-up at room temperature (23 ± 1 ◦C). (Fig. S1). The freestanding GOMs were installed between the upstream and downstream compartments of a cell (Side-Bi-Side Cells, PermeGear), providing an effective membrane area of 2.54 cm2. The downstream chamber contained 40 mL of deionized water and a conductivity probe (SevenExcellence S700, Mettler Toledo), while the upstream chamber was filled with 40 mL of the relevant salt solution.

The ionic conductivity of the GOMs were thus measured using a 4-compartment electrochemical cell (Fig. S2) at room tempera­ture (23 ± 1 ◦C). A membrane with an effective membrane area of 3.14 cm2 was positioned between the compartments, each filled with the same concentration of salt solution, which was continuously recircu­lated. Ag/AgCl reference electrodes filled with 3 M KCl were positioned near the membrane surface using Luggins capillaries and utilized to record the voltage response at a given current using an electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments 1140C using multi-current step mode). The membrane ionic resistance (Rm) was determined by subtracting the solution resistance (Rs) from the total measured resistance (Rm + s) [62]. The results reported represent the average of at least four replicate membranes per condition. The ionic conductivity (κ) was then estimated by the following equation [63]: κ =LRm*AEq. 7 where L is the membrane thickness and A is the effective membrane area. An alternative method was also used to confirm this data using a twoelectrode potentiometric electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) method, with an AC potential bias of 10 mV and a frequency range of 0.2 MHz–10 Hz. GOMs were soaked in aqueous electrolyte solution for 24 h prior to measurement.

Statistical significance was determined by paired t-tests and linear regression. 3. Results 3.1. Water uptake Water volume fraction, a critical parameter in hydrated membranes, governs ion transport behaviour [66]. The water uptake for single salts has been discussed in detail in our previous study [32]. The hydrated membrane thicknesses for both single and binary salts are shown in Fig. 1A, while the water uptake results are presented in Fig. 1B. Both show a decreasing trend with increasing external salt concentration, attributed to osmotic deswelling [67,68]. The binary salt results generally fall between the values of the corresponding single salt solu­tions, consistent with observations in polymeric membranes [69].

In the binary systems containing K+ ions, the water uptake and hydrated membrane thickness values tend to shift toward those of the K+ ion single salt systems, indicating that K+ ions may play a dominant role in governing water uptake and hydrated membrane structures. Prior studies have employed K+ ions to stabilise GO films or adjust GO morphology [70,71] and such application may inherently relate to its influence on membrane hydration. Since partitioning and water uptake in GOMs are thermodynamically governed by the chemical potential of the solution, the data in Fig. 1were also plotted as a function of solution activity (Fig. S3). The trends observed were similar when plotted on this basis.

content: 3.2. Ion sorption Both counterand co-ion concentrations in GOMs were measured. While detailed results for single salt systems have been reported previ­ously [32], this study extends the analysis by including binary salt sys­tems. All comparative results are presented in Fig. 2, reported as equivalents of sorbed ions per litre of swollen membrane as a function of external salt concentration. In all binary systems, increasing the external salt concentration led to higher sorbed ion concentrations. For counter-ions, concentrations in­side the membrane exceeded those in the external solution, corre­sponding to a partition coefficient greater than one. In contrast, Cl-ion partition coefficients were close to unity across all conditions. It is worth noting that these values are based on concentration units of moles per litre of swollen membrane. In our prior work, co-ion partition co­efficients above unity were observed, when measured with concentra­tion units of moles per litre of water sorbed. In all cases, total cation concentrations exceeded those of co-ions, although the difference was not statistically significant at higher external salt concentrations due to weaker Donnan exclusion [72]. In the LiCl + NaCl system (Fig. 2A), Li+ and Na+ ion concentrations were nearly identical at all analysed concentrations (p > 0.05), indi­cating negligible competitive sorption between these cations. In contrast, in other monovalent binary systems (Fig.

In contrast, in other monovalent binary systems (Fig. 2B and C), K+ ion concentrations were consistently higher than those of the accompanying cations (p < 0.05 for both LiCl + KCl and NaCl + KCl), suggesting a preferential sorption of K+ ions and consistent with the water uptake results. This selectivity may arise from the smaller hydrated radius of the K+ ion [73] and its stronger interaction with oxygen-functional groups on GO. Additionally, K+ ions exhibits stronger π-cation interactions with sp2 clusters within the GO [74]. Klímov´a et al. also reported that higher sorption correlates with the larger ionic radii and greater coordination ability of larger ions [75]. Unlike Na+ ions, whose higher hydration energy leads to weaker interactions with GO, K+ ions form stronger associations. Mo et al. further highlighted that smaller hydrated radii protect the ionic structure, limiting the interaction between ions and GO in the case of Na+ ions, but not for K+ ions [76]. content: 3.3. Salt permeability The salt permeability coefficients (Ps) for a range of salts were determined through concentration gradient driven analysis using solu­tions of LiCl, NaCl, KCl, and MgCl2 and equimolar binary monovalent salt solutions. In single salt solutions, the results show that LiCl has the highest permeability, followed by NaCl, MgCl2 and KCl (Fig. 3 and Fig. S5). However, the differences between the different salts are small at higher salt concentrations (1 eq/L), with all tending to converge to a single value of around 1.8 * 10-11 m2/s. Our permeabilities are generally higher than those reported in most GO studies with a maximum deviation of seven-fold (Fig. 3). These differences in permeability may stem from methodological differences in membrane fabrication. We employed a doctor blade technique, similar to the drop-casting approach used by Sun et al. who achieved results comparable to the present study [7]. Other studies primarily use vacuum filtration [3,8,31], which tends to yield denser membranes with lower permeability. Other factors could also contribute to the differ­ences in salt permeability, such as the number of oxygen functional groups and interlayer spacing [31,80,81]. Differences in the oxygen functional group density influence membrane hydrophilicity and ion sorption behaviour, ultimately affecting salt permeability.

content: fferences in the oxygen functional group density influence membrane hydrophilicity and ion sorption behaviour, ultimately affecting salt permeability. Compared to polymeric membranes, our NaCl permeability co­efficients, ranging from 1.66 * 10-11 to 2.81 * 10-11 m2/s exceed those of polyamide-based membranes [79] and the FKS-30 cation exchange membrane [77], both of which have low water uptake values (e.g., 0.22 g/g for FKS-30). However, as the NaCl concentration rises to 1 M, the water uptake of our GO membrane falls to 0.5 g/g, below the 0.8 g/g observed for CR-61 [82]. This reduction in hydration could explain why our permeability coefficient falls below that of CR-61 at 1 M. In the case of MgCl2, however, our permeability coefficients are similar to or lower than those of CR-61, despite the GO membrane having higher or com­parable water uptake. In the binary mixture of LiCl and NaCl, the overall salt permeability falls between those of the individual salts across all tested concentra­tions (Fig. S5A). Similar behaviour is observed in the Li+- K+ ion and Na+- K+ ion systems: initial permeability values fall between those of the respective single salts but gradually approach that of KCl as the con­centration increases. At 1 M, the binary salt permeability for mixtures containing LiCl with KCl or NaCl with KCl is even lower than the values observed for the corresponding single-salt systems (p < 0.05).

Cation permeability coefficients in binary systems, estimated using Eq. (3), show that Li+ and Na+ ions have nearly identical values in the Li+- Na+ ion mixtures (Fig. S5B and D). In contrast, K+ ions consistently exhibit higher permeability than the accompanying cation in the other systems, indicating stronger permselectivity for K+ ions. This trend is consistent with the partitioning selectivity patterns observed earlier in ion sorption (Fig. 2) and the water uptake results (Fig. 1B). Once the salt permeability coefficient and solubility are known, the salt diffusion coefficient can be readily calculated based on the solution-diffusion model (Ps = Ks * Ds) [83]. The resulting salt diffusion coefficients for single and binary salt solution systems are presented in Fig. S6. How­ever, the primary focus of this study remains on the individual ion diffusion coefficients.

content: 3.4. Water permeability The water permeability coefficient values determined experimen­tally are 0.21, 0.32, and 0.09 L μm/(hr bar m2) for 1 M LiCl, NaCl and KCl, respectively (permeance values of 0.052, 0.011 and 0.0054 L/m2 h bar), with the lower value for KCl reflecting the reduced water uptake. These measurements were derived under purely osmotic driving forces, without any externally applied hydraulic pressure. Direct comparison with literature values, which often report very high water permeance (up to 71 L/m2 h bar) is difficult, as these studies typically involve composite membranes with ultrathin GO layers [84–87] and exhibit significant variation due to differences in fabrication methods, testing conditions, and membrane stability [88]. However, as one example, a 70 nm thick GO layer would provide a water permeability of 1–5 L/m2 h bar based on the current results, whereas we recorded water perme­abilities of 31 L/m2 h bar for a commercial composite membrane with a GO layer of this thickness [89]. This suggests that in larger scale GOMs, the water permeability is enhanced by flow through defects in the GO sheet structure. GOMs are also known to show a dynamic microstruc­ture, where loosely packed flakes can cause transiently high initial fluxes that are not intrinsic, with the steady-state water permeance in pressure-driven filtration generally low due to the extremely high tor­tuosity of the membrane structure [88].

The relatively low water permeability values indicate that the os­motic water transport through the freestanding GOMs is minimal under the studied conditions, resulting in only 1 to 2 % differences between the corrected and uncorrected salt diffusion coefficients. Therefore, the impact of osmotic water flux on salt transport is considered negligible under the experimental conditions of this study.

content: 3.5. Membrane resistance and ionic conductivity Membrane resistance was determined using three independent techniques. The use of either DC or AC in the four-compartment cell gave identical values within the margins of experimental error, even at con­centrations of 0.1 M (Fig. S7). Conversely, the use of AC in the coin cell apparatus generally showed higher membrane resistances than when using the four-compartment cell (Fig. S8B). We speculate that the dif­ference may arise from the extent to which the membranes were com­pressed during the measurement process. In the four-compartment cell, the membranes were free standing. Conversely, in the coin cell approach the electrodes were clamped to the membrane. This is likely to compress the fragile membranes, forcing the GO sheets closer together and causing an increase in resistance and ultimately reducing membrane conductivity [90,91]. For this reason, we used the four-compartment DC measurements in the remainder of this study. In general, our ionic conductivity measurements determined in the four-compartment cell gave higher values than reported in the literature for GO membranes measured using an AC approach (see Supporting Information Section S4). These differences may relate to both the mea­surement technique as discussed above, as well as the methods used to prepare the membranes. As noted earlier, other studies primarily use vacuum filtration, which tends to yield denser membranes.

When comparing our results with those for polymeric membranes, the ionic conductivity of the GOMs, estimated by the four-compartment DC method, was 8.6 mS/cm in 1 M NaCl, which aligns with previously re­ported values for 40 different polymeric membranes in Espinoza et al. In all cases, the membrane resistance for single salt solutions remained essentially constant for each salt above an external salt con­centration of 0.5 M (Fig. 4A and Fig. S8). However, as the external salt concentrations decreased, a significant increase in resistance was observed. This aligns with observations reported by previous re­searchers [62,77,93–101]. When the external concentration falls below a critical threshold (i.e., <0.1 M), the membrane resistance primarily arises from the aqueous phase, making it comparable to the resistance of the surrounding solution. The space-charge model provides a comple­mentary perspective, suggesting that at low salt concentrations, trans­port is dominated by flow through the centre of the nanochannels, where the local viscosity is lower, rather than through interactions with the fixed charge groups [102]. Considering the stacked-channel struc­ture of GOMs, this mechanism is likely a dominant factor influencing the higher resistance observed under dilute conditions.

Other studies sug­gest that the increased resistance at low salt concentrations is due to the increasing influence of the electrical double layer resistance and diffu­sion boundary layer resistance [62]. In the DC experiments, however, resistance was measured across varied flow rates (from 130 to 530 mL/min), with negligible differences observed suggesting that the impact of the diffusion boundary layer on our results is minimal [62]. In binary salt systems, the membrane resistance was consistently higher than that observed for the corresponding single-salt solutions, leading to lower ionic conductivity across all external salt concentra­tions (Fig. 4). This behaviour deviates from the commonly reported trend [103–105], where binary system conductivity typically falls be­tween those of the individual salts. However, similar deviations have been reported in certain polymeric membranes [106]. The underlying cause of this behaviour in freestanding GOMs remains unclear and warrants further investigation. 3.6. (5)), pro­vided the concentrations of co-ions and counter-ions per litre of swollen membrane are known. The counter-ion diffusivities for Mg2+, K+, and Li+ are independent of external salt concentration (p > 0.05) while that for Na+ shows a slight decline (Fig. 5A). Most studies of polymeric membranes similarly show that counterion diffusivity remains essentially independent of external salt concentration [77,107,108]. This observation aligns with previous findings reported for sulfonated polymers such as XLAMPS and HQ:BP-XX [109], as well as certain ion-exchange membranes [52,108]. Kamcev et al. attribute this trend to a balance between two opposing effects that occur at high external salt concentrations: (1) osmotic deswelling caused by reduced water content, which decreases the vol­ume available for ion transport and could lower ion diffusion co­efficients, and (2) increased ion sorption due to weakened Donnan exclusion, which raises counter-ion concentrations to maintain electro­neutrality [52]. These opposing effects may effectively negate each other, resulting in a negligible net change in Dgm. Our findings suggest a similar cancellation of effects, leading to the observed constancy in counter-ion diffusivity [107].

content: ligible net change in Dgm. Our findings suggest a similar cancellation of effects, leading to the observed constancy in counter-ion diffusivity [107]. Conversely, other studies [77,110] sug­gest that osmotic deswelling may reduce the distance between fixed charged groups, thereby accelerating counter-ion transport and increasing the diffusion coefficient by facilitating more efficient ion hopping [77,110]. In this mechanism, the denser membrane structure brings charged sites closer together, allowing ions to move more rapidly between them despite the limited free volume. Our estimated K+ ion diffusion coefficient (12 ± 3 * 10-11 m2/s) is approximately six times higher than the reported data (2.1 ± 0.3 *10-11 m2/s) by Eliseev et al. for GO in 1 M KCl, while our Mg2+ ion value (0.85 ± 0.2 * 10-11 m2/s) is in close agreement with their reported value (0.84 ± 0.2 * 10-11 m2/s) for GO in 1 M MgCl2 [15]. Moreover, our results align well with the range determined by Abraham et al. through MD simulations [3]. Specifically, they reported K+ ion diffusion coefficients ranging from 4.9 * 10-11 to 96 * 10-11 m2/s in GO with interlayer spacings between 0.7 nm and 1.1 nm. For reduced graphene oxide (rGO), the K+ ion diffusion coefficient values varied between 3.5 * 10-11 and 1.5 * 10-11 m2/s, depending on interlayer spacing (4.5 nm–10.9 nm) and surface charge density (0 to -56 mC/m2) [27].

Our measured diffusion coefficient falls within these ranges, suggesting that the structural characteristics of our GO system may facilitate similar ion transport dynamics as those observed in rGO materials. Our K+ ion diffusion coefficient is nearly 16 times lower than the value for a bulk solution, and for the Mg2+ ions it is approximately 85 times lower than in a bulk solution [111] ( see also Table S3 for com­parison). These significant reductions indicate restricted ion mobility within GOMs compared to free diffusion in bulk liquids. The average counter-ion diffusion coefficients in the GOMs nanochannels follow the order: Li+ > Na+ > K+ > Mg2+. This trend aligns with the pattern observed in the hydrated diameters of monovalent ions (Table S3) and their water uptake values but contrasts with the order of their self-diffusion coefficients, where smaller ions like Li+ ions typically have lower mobility in bulk solution due to their larger hydration shells [112,113]. Li+ ions, with the highest hydration energy [3], can maintain a strongly bound hydration shell [114], reducing interactions with the membrane and allowing these ions to diffuse more freely. In contrast, K+ ions, with lower hydration energy and larger size, experience stronger interactions with the unmodified graphene sheet and the oxygen

In the case of the Mg2+ ion, the results show significantly lower diffusivity due to its divalent nature (Fig. 5A), which results in stronger ion-membrane interactions with the membrane fixed charges [74], limiting ion mobility. Additionally, the larger size of the Mg2+ ion further contrib­utes to its reduced mobility within the membrane [73,116]. The magnitude of our Na+ ion diffusion coefficient is comparable, or slightly higher, than those reported for polymeric membranes (Fig. S9A) despite often having higher water content (Fig. 1B). The GOM’s narrow, tortuous nanochannels create significant resistance to ion movement [31], limiting effective diffusion pathways even in a hydrated environ­ment. Moreover, studies have found that strong electrostatic, van der Waals, and hydrogen bond interactions between the hydrated ions and the oxygen-containing groups in GOMs further hinder mass transport and contribute to the limited mobility of species within the membrane [117]. Furthermore, confined water in the graphitic regions of the GOMs nanochannels hinders ion movement by increasing friction, as the hy­dration shells interact with the channel walls. Wen et al.

content: 3.7. Co-ion diffusivity The diffusion coefficient of the chloride co-ions is more consistent between the four salts, as might be expected given it is the same ion but still shows some variation (Fig. 5B). This variation can be explained by changes in the d-spacing and water uptake. For example, the membrane has a greater water uptake and larger hydrated thickness when placed in an LiCl solution and thus the diffusion coefficient of Cl-ions in this solution is greater. Similarly, the water uptake and hydrated thickness is lowest for KCl at external concentrations of greater than 0.5 M, consis­tent with the Cl-ion diffusivities shown. The values are within a com­parable range to that for polymeric membranes (Fig. S9B). To the best of our knowledge, the Cl-ion diffusion coefficient in GOMs has not been previously reported in the literature. The Cl-ion diffusivity increases with external salt concentration up to 0.3 M for both KCl and MgCl2 and then stabilizes for all salt solutions, mirroring the trend observed in our ionic conductivity measurements (Figs. 4B and 5B). The increase in diffusion coefficient as external salt concentration decreased is somewhat surprising given the large reduc­tion in water uptake that simultaneously occurs in these GOMs. In polymeric membranes, such increased water uptake at low external salt concentrations typically increases diffusivity [66] such as observed by

[109], however, observe an increase in co-ion diffu­sivity at lower solution concentrations for a sulfonated cation exchange polymer (HQ:BP-XX) while the counter ion concentration is constant. This is consistent with the present results. These authors argue that repulsive interactions between fixed charge groups and co-ions can in­crease the effective free volume required for diffusion by reducing thermodynamic favorability, as larger separations minimize electro­static repulsion. The ratio of the counterand co-ion diffusion coefficients in bulk solution (Dgs/Dcs) was consistently lower than in GOMs (Dgm/Dcm) for all monovalent ions (Fig. 7). That is, counter-ions move significantly faster than co-ions within the membrane phase, whereas counter-ions move more slowly than their chloride co-ions in solution. Conversely, for the divalent MgCl2, the diffusion coefficient ratios in the solution (Table S3) and inside the membrane (Fig. 5) remain relatively comparable, with ratios still higher in the membrane, and co-ions moving faster than the counter-ions except at low external concentration (e.g., 0.01 M).

These results suggest that ion diffusion in GOMs is influenced more by specific ion-membrane interactions, such as electrostatic effects, rather than by ion size alone. These findings highlight the significant influence of ion size, charge, and specific interactions with the membrane on ion diffu­sion behaviour in GOMs. 3.8. Ion diffusion coefficients in monovalent binary systems No direct and simple method is available to determine the individual ion diffusion coefficients in the binary salt systems [72,118,119]. To overcome this, average diffusion coefficients were calculated for cations using their overall concentrations in the membrane using Eq. (5) and Eq. (6), with the results shown in Fig. 8. The estimated average counter-ion diffusion coefficients in binary systems were generally lower than those in single-salt systems, except at low external concentrations (e.g., 0.01 M). On the contrary, Cl-ion diffusion coefficients in binary systems fell between the values obtained for single-salt systems, within the range of experimental uncertainty (Fig. S10). 4. Conclusions In this study, individual ion diffusion coefficients in GOMs across varied external salt concentrations (LiCl, NaCl, KCl, and MgCl2) were experimentally estimated using diffusion cell and conductivity experi­ments, combined with solubility data from our prior research. The re­sults revealed that counter-ion diffusion coefficients are independent of external salt concentration, while co-ion diffusion coefficients increase with concentration up to ~0.3 M before stabilising. Notably, Na+ and Cl-ion diffusion coefficients in GOMs are comparable to those of polymeric membranes, challenging the notion of rapid ion transport in GOMs as previously reported. The findings emphasise that ion perme­ability in GOMs is primarily driven by solubility effects rather than diffusivity. Recorded water permeability values were also low, again challenging our understanding of transport through these systems.

content: cts rather than diffusivity. Recorded water permeability values were also low, again challenging our understanding of transport through these systems. Compared to single-salt systems, binary mixtures generally exhibited lower counter-ion diffusion coefficients, particularly at higher external concentrations. In contrast, Cl-ion diffusion coefficients remained within the range of single-salt values, following trends observed in membrane water uptake. These findings suggest that monovalent binary ion environments introduce additional transport complexity, especially for counter-ions, and highlight the influence of membrane hydration on co-ion mobility. Further work is needed to fully understand the under­lying mechanisms governing ion transport in multicomponent systems. The work establishes a foundation for understanding ion transport mechanisms in GOMs, including those equilibrated with monovalent binary salt systems, offering insights into their potential applications in electrochemical energy conversion and storage, water treatment, and environmental systems. By elucidating the distinct role of solubility and electrostatic interactions, this study contributes to the advancement of tailored membrane technologies for a wide range of scientific and in­dustrial applications.
